evidence: All major adapters keep a dual `on_message` surface (`ChatMessageTurnContext` plus legacy arg-bag fields) and immediately normalize/unpack (`src/thenvoi/core/simple_adapter.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/parlant.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/claude_sdk.py`).

`src/thenvoi/core/protocols.py` defines many layered tool protocols, while conformance testing in `tests/core/test_protocols.py` validates one monolithic dummy against nearly all protocol variants, indicating high interface fan-out without independent implementer pressure.

A2A gateway keeps parallel peer abstractions (`_peer_directory`, `_peers`, `_peers_by_uuid`) and tests mutate private backing maps directly (`src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a/gateway/adapter.py`, `tests/integration/test_a2a_gateway_context.py`), showing leakage of internal storage choices into usage/testing.
`src/thenvoi/runtime/types.py` is primarily dataclasses, enums, constants, and two small normalization helpers, which lowers direct behavioral risk compared to stateful logic.

`ContactEventConfig.__post_init__` contains runtime validation branches (CALLBACK requires `on_event`; callback must be async), but this file does not embed executable checks/examples that verify edge callable shapes over time.

The module includes API-shaping helpers (`normalize_handle`, `is_agent_sender_type`, `PlatformMessage.format_for_llm`) that are simple but contract-sensitive for downstream behavior, so targeted contract tests are still valuable.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: Validation and formatting contracts are present but not self-verifying in this module; adding explicit contract tests for callback validation and normalization/formatting edge cases would increase confidence.

evidence: Adapter chat entrypoints repeatedly normalize the same legacy argument bag pattern (`normalize_chat_turn`) instead of centralizing migration handling in one dispatch path (`src/thenvoi/core/simple_adapter.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/claude_sdk.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/crewai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`).

Tool execution reporting (`tool_call`/`tool_result`) and payload-shaping policy are implemented independently across adapter modules and integration tool surfaces (`src/thenvoi/adapters/anthropic.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/pydantic_ai.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/langgraph.py`, `src/thenvoi/adapters/codex.py`, `src/thenvoi/integrations/claude_sdk/tools.py`, `src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py`).

Gateway and runtime modules contain duplicated structural representations of the same state/contract (peer caches mirrored beside `PeerDirectory`; duplicated tool-order registries and duplicated arg-default logic in bridge dispatch paths) (`src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a/gateway/adapter.py`, `src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a/gateway/server.py`, `src/thenvoi/runtime/tools.py`, `src/thenvoi/runtime/tool_bridge.py`).

Example and test harnesses duplicate orchestration scaffolding with drift between sibling flows (`examples/run_agent.py` vs `src/thenvoi/testing/runner_core.py`; `docker/claude_sdk/runner.py` vs `examples/claude_sdk_docker/runner.py`; duplicated dynamic-agent setup across integration tests).

findings.summary: Adapters preserve two `on_message` contracts at once, forcing repetitive normalization/unpacking and weakening a single honest interface.
evidence: `SimpleAdapter.normalize_chat_turn` explicitly supports both context-style and legacy arg-bag invocation.
Each adapter `on_message` starts with `turn = self.normalize_chat_turn(...)` then repeats `msg = turn.msg`, `tools = turn.tools`, `history = turn.history` unpack boilerplate.
The same compatibility shape appears across framework adapters rather than being isolated to a migration boundary.
findings.suggestion: Move to a single `ChatMessageTurnContext`/`ControlMessageTurnContext` entrypoint, keep legacy support in one compatibility shim/decorator with a time-boxed deprecation plan, and remove per-adapter unpack scaffolding after callers/tests are migrated.

findings.summary: Tool protocol hierarchy is over-segmented relative to observed usage, increasing type indirection and maintenance without clear extension payoff.
evidence: `core/protocols.py` defines multiple layered protocols (`MessagingToolsProtocol`, `ParticipantToolsProtocol`, `ChatToolsProtocol`, `ContactToolsProtocol`, `MemoryToolsProtocol`, `PlatformToolOperationsProtocol`, `ToolSchemaProviderProtocol`, `ToolDispatchProtocol`, and composed variants).
`tests/core/test_protocols.py` uses one `_DummyTools` implementation and asserts conformance to almost all protocol layers, suggesting weak independent boundaries.

Adapters typically type against broad composed protocols anyway (`MessagingDispatchToolsProtocol`, `AnthropicSchemaToolsProtocol`), so many intermediary layers add conceptual hops more than isolation.
findings.suggestion: Collapse protocol layers to a smaller set aligned to real adapter seams (e.g., dispatch-only, dispatch+schema, full tools), and keep narrower interfaces only where a distinct implementation/caller pair exists.

findings.summary: A2A gateway exposes multiple peer state representations, and tests depend on private map mutation, indicating abstraction leakage and dual source-of-truth risk.
evidence: Adapter stores peer data in `_peer_directory` and also mirrors to `_peers` and `_peers_by_uuid`.
State is reassigned from directory (`self._peers = self._peer_directory.peers`, `self._peers_by_uuid = self._peer_directory.peers_by_uuid`) but tests directly set `_peers_by_uuid` private state.
Direct private-map coupling in tests shows callers are forced to know storage details instead of using a stable query API.
findings.suggestion: Make `PeerDirectory` the sole peer store, expose explicit read methods for test/runtime use, and remove mutable mirror maps (or make them read-only views) so consumers cannot bypass the canonical directory.

findings.summary: Example bootstrap/runner helpers form a broad wrapper surface that mostly forwards configuration to `Agent.create`, creating indirection without strong domain policy.
evidence: `bootstrap.py` chains thin wrappers (`load_runtime_config` -> `create_agent_from_runtime` -> `bootstrap_agent`) around URL/credential resolution and `Agent.create`.
`example_runners.py` repeats near-identical adapter construction and `Agent.create` orchestration across many `run_*_agent` functions with minor parameter variation.
Most wrappers are pass-through composition points rather than bounded behavior units, increasing maintenance touchpoints when runtime creation contracts change.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a small declarative runner spec (adapter factory + per-framework option transform) and one generic launch path; keep only truly framework-specific pre/post hooks as dedicated functions.

findings.summary: Quality-gate claims are broader than enforceable contracts visible in repo governance config
evidence: README.md declares required contract checks for bootstrap contracts and prompt contracts.
pyproject.toml defines a `contract_gate` marker string but no explicit governance map tying those claims to concrete target files/contracts.

High-importer contract-sensitive files (`examples/common/bootstrap.py`, `examples/prompts/characters.py`) do not expose a versioned contract artifact or manifest in scope that CI can verify for completeness.
findings.suggestion: Add a contract manifest (for example `tests/contracts/manifest.yaml`) that explicitly maps each claimed contract area to concrete test modules and covered files, and add a CI check that fails if README quality-gate claims and manifest entries drift.

findings.summary: Integration cleanup strategy can hide real failures and increase flaky outcomes
evidence: cleanup_contact_state in `tests/support/integration/config.py` records recoverable failures and, when best-effort is enabled, logs a warning then returns instead of failing the test run.
The same fixture layer uses bounded polling (`settle_timeout_s=5.0`) and repeated sleep-based retries for eventual consistency, which is environment-sensitive.
README.md presents this cleanup as a stability policy, but current behavior allows partial cleanup failure to pass, reducing confidence in isolation guarantees.
findings.suggestion: Split cleanup outcomes into strict vs advisory modes at marker level: keep best-effort only for explicitly labeled stress tests, and make default integration paths fail on any cleanup failure with structured diagnostics for each failed step.

findings.summary: Integration marker policy depends on duplicated declarations with no sync guard
evidence: `tests/support/integration/markers.py` composes runtime skip decorators from `INTEGRATION_MARKER_SPECS` and specific env-var assumptions (including first-item indexing for single-env markers).
`pyproject.toml` separately declares marker taxonomy (`requires_api`, `requires_multi_agent`, `requires_user_api`, `contract_gate`).
README.md claims integration marker policy is part of required quality gates, but no in-scope guard proves marker declarations and runtime skip policy remain synchronized.
findings.suggestion: Add a contract-gate test that imports marker decorators and asserts one-to-one parity with `pyproject.toml` marker definitions plus expected env requirements, so marker naming or requirement drift fails CI immediately.

findings.summary: Legacy call-shape normalization is duplicated across adapters instead of being fully owned by base dispatch.
evidence: `SimpleAdapter.normalize_chat_turn` is defined as a compatibility normalization point, but adapter `chat(...)` methods still expose broad legacy params and manually unpack the normalized turn.

The same normalize-then-reassign structure appears in multiple adapters (`anthropic.py:140`, `claude_sdk.py:351`, `crewai.py:446`, `pydantic_ai.py:218`), indicating migration concerns are spread into provider implementations.
findings.suggestion: Move legacy signature support into a single base-level `chat` entrypoint (or decorator) that always passes a canonical `ChatTurn` into provider-specific methods; simplify adapter signatures to provider concerns only.

findings.summary: Tool-call/tool-result reporting policy is reimplemented per adapter, causing repeated structure and behavior drift risk.
evidence: Each adapter/tooling module contains its own logic for emitting `tool_call` and `tool_result` events and handling reporting failures.

Repeated event-shaping and best-effort error handling patterns appear across modules (`anthropic.py:418+`, `pydantic_ai.py:292+`, `langgraph.py:266+`, `codex.py:896+`, `crewai_tooling.py:80+`, `claude_sdk.py:525+`).
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared runtime reporting component (input: tool name/args/result/error, execution context) and invoke it from adapters; keep adapters focused on framework integration points.

findings.summary: Claude and Parlant integrations maintain parallel tool catalogs and formatting rules rather than one canonical contract source.
evidence: Both modules define overlapping tool surfaces (`thenvoi_send_message`, `thenvoi_send_event`, participant/contact tools) and adapter-specific mapping logic.
The duplicated contract appears as independent schema/description/mapping implementations (`claude_sdk/tools.py:214+`, `parlant/tools.py:183+`, `parlant/tools.py:423+`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a framework-agnostic canonical tool descriptor model in runtime, then generate Claude/Parlant-specific shapes from that model via thin translators.

findings.summary: Tool argument defaults and tool ordering are duplicated between runtime modules, creating synchronization debt.
evidence: `tool_bridge.py` maintains `PLATFORM_BASE_TOOL_ORDER`/`PLATFORM_MEMORY_TOOL_ORDER` while `tools.py` already defines `TOOL_MODELS` and derived tool-name sets; runtime assertions enforce sync after duplication (`tool_bridge.py:45-48`).
`normalize_tool_arguments` and `dispatch_platform_tool_call` both apply tool-specific default/coercion behavior in separate branches (`tool_bridge.py:167+` and `tool_bridge.py:242+`).
findings.suggestion: Derive order and argument contracts from one canonical registry (e.g., metadata attached to `TOOL_MODELS`) and have dispatch consume normalized arguments from a single normalization pass.

findings.summary: A2A gateway components keep mirrored peer caches alongside `PeerDirectory`, creating split state ownership.
evidence: Adapter keeps `_peers` and `_peers_by_uuid` dicts plus `_peer_directory`, then reassigns mirror fields from directory state (`adapter.py:104-106`, `adapter.py:151-153`).
Server similarly manages peer-directory responsibilities, increasing the chance of divergence across gateway-side peer lifecycle paths.
findings.suggestion: Make `PeerDirectory` the sole mutable store and expose read-only projections where required; remove mirrored caches and route lookups through directory APIs.

findings.summary: Runner orchestration logic is duplicated across example and docker entrypoints, with behavioral drift between sibling runners.
evidence: `examples/run_agent.py` defines its own `RunnerSpec` and runner-selection orchestration while `runner_core.py` already provides shared runner contracts and execution primitives.

Claude docker runner and example docker runner are near-parallel entrypoints, but only docker variant performs repo initialization/context bundle injection (`docker/claude_sdk/runner.py:76`, `:102`) while example variant omits it.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate runner wiring through `runner_core` + shared composable hooks; keep each entrypoint as minimal configuration, and encode optional repo-context behavior as explicit feature flags.

findings.summary: Integration tests duplicate dynamic-agent lifecycle scaffolding and multi-step setup flows instead of reusing shared fixtures.
evidence: Both `test_dynamic_agent.py` and `test_participant_permissions.py` define their own `DynamicAgent` data structures and custom lifecycle management setup.
`test_hub_room_strategy.py` repeats approve/reject orchestration blocks with mostly identical setup and only outcome branching.
findings.suggestion: Extract a common dynamic-agent fixture/factory module for integration tests and parameterize approval/rejection scenario variants on top of shared setup helpers.

findings.summary: Runtime components use inconsistent nonfatal error recording patterns, fragmenting operational behavior.
evidence: `CodexWebSocketClient` uses `NonFatalErrorRecorder`, while `ThenvoiLink` and `RoomPresence` implement local `_nonfatal_errors` and bespoke `_record_nonfatal_error` methods.
Sibling codex transports differ in error-recording structure (`websocket_client.py` recorder-based vs `stdio_client.py` ad-hoc handling), indicating boundary inconsistency within one subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one runtime nonfatal recorder abstraction (mixin or composed helper) and apply it consistently across link/presence/transports with unified metadata shape.
